vscc.q: Analyse and evaluate the enterprise problem your DSS intends to solve and clearly justify why it requires a DSS solution.
vscc.subject: Look at the NESA verbs (write all verbs you find)
vscc.criteria: Analyse key features of existing systems and use a criteria (ease of use/complexity/speed/quality) to evaluate the existing systems for any limitations to justify why the problem exist and requires a solution.
vscc.content: what features of existing solytions you will evaluate and justify they need a solution

vscc.q: Describe the overall purpose of your DSS.
vscc.subject: The purpose and the goal of DSS
vscc.criteria: Describe the intent of the system , Decisions the system supports, target audience and recommendations produced.
vscc.content: problem addresses, target users, decision making tasks, types of recommendations

vscc.q: Analyse the needs of the target users.
vscc.subject: The needs and requirements of the target users.
vscc.criteria: Examine how user needs e.g. User goals, problems, technical expertise,constraints, access to information can influence DSS design.

vscc.q: Evaluate the suitability of the decisions or recommendations the system will produce.
vscc.subject: Decisions or recommendations produced by the DSS
vscc.criteria: Considering both advantages and limitations to evaluate the outputs against defined quality criteria sich as accuracy, usefulness,speed,reliability,clarity
vscc.content: Think about what quality criteria will be used to evaluate the outputs generated by your DSS and how would you Evaluate it.

vscc.q: Use a top-level IPSO diagram to illustrate how the system will function including knowledge base, rule base, inference process and user inputs.
vscc.subject: The conceptual functioning of the DSS
vscc.criteria: IPSO should clearly show inputs, processes, storage and outputs through labelling and annotations identifying all parts of the DSS and how data flows from different parts to generate recommendations/outputs. Add a brief explanantion about the diagram and how the system works.
vscc.content: IPSO diagram, labelling, annotation, data flow, explanation

vscc.q: Analyse and evaluate the types of data to be used.
vscc.subject: The data used witin the DSS
vscc.criteria: Analyse multiple kind of data sources and their types (structured/unstructued/alternate) and evaluate their suitablity to generate outputs.
vscc.content: data types, data sources, criteria to assess suitability, WHY chosen data is suitable to generate outputs from DSS

vscc.q: Evaluate relevant social, ethical and legal considerations related to data collection, processing and system design.
vscc.subject: Social, Ethcal and legal implications of data collection and DSS design
vscc.criteria: Evaluate considerations related to data collection,processing and system design features which have social, ethical and legal implications.
vscc.content: impact on users, accessibility, fairness of decisions, transparency of system logic, data bias, privacy laws, data protection, IP issues

build.q: Analyse and evaluate the enterprise problem your DSS intends to solve and clearly justify why it requires a DSS solution.

steps.label: Context/Problem Identification
steps.hint: Introduce the enterprise problem and explain where it occurs.
steps.starter: The enterprise problem that this Decision Support System aims to address is…

steps.label: Background Explanation
steps.hint: Explain how this problem currently affects users or organisations.
steps.starter: Currently, users experience this problem because…

steps.label: Analysis of existing solutions
steps.hint: Examine how similar systems are attempting to solve the problem
steps.starter: Several existing systems attempt to solve this problem, such as…

steps.label: Feature Analysis
steps.hint: Break down the features of these systems and how they function. [use references+images+Other data to support your identification of problem]
steps.starter: These systems typically include features such as…

steps.label: Evaluation Against Criteria
steps.hint: Evaluate these systems using criteria like ease of use, complexity or speed. [Design your own metrics/Criteria] Collect evidence (user reviews etc)
steps.starter: When evaluated against criteria such as ease of use, accuracy and response time, these systems…

steps.label: Justified Judgement
steps.hint: Explain why these limitations justify the need for your DSS.
steps.starter: Therefore, a new DSS is justified because it will…

build.q: Describe the overall purpose of your DSS.

steps.label: System Introduction
steps.hint: Introduce the DSS and its main purpose.
steps.starter: The purpose of this Decision Support System is to…

steps.label: Target Users
steps.hint: Identify who will use the system.
steps.starter: The primary users of this system are…

steps.label: Decision Support Function
steps.hint: Explain what decision the system supports.
steps.starter: The system assists users in making decisions about…

steps.label: System Outputs
steps.hint: Explain what recommendations or outputs the system will produce.
steps.starter: Based on user inputs and system rules, the DSS will generate…

build.q: Analyse the needs of the target users.

steps.label: Identify Users
steps.hint: Introduce the target users.
steps.starter: The target users of this DSS include…

steps.label: Describe User Goals
steps.hint: Explain what users want to achieve.
steps.starter: These users need to make decisions about…

steps.label: Analyse User Challenges
steps.hint: Break down the problems users face without the system. [This is a summary information from Q1]
steps.starter: One major challenge users experience is…

steps.label: Analyse Information Needs
steps.hint: Explain the type of information users need to make decisions.
steps.starter: To make effective decisions, users require access to…

steps.label: Link Needs to DSS Features
steps.hint: Explain how the system design addresses these needs.
steps.starter: The DSS addresses these needs by…

build.q: Evaluate the suitability of the decisions or recommendations the system will produce.

steps.label: Identify System Decisions
steps.hint: Explain the type of recommendations the system produces.
steps.starter: The DSS will generate recommendations such as…

steps.label: Explain How Decisions Are Generated
steps.hint: Describe how rules and data lead to these recommendations.
steps.starter: These recommendations are generated using…

steps.label: Analyse Decision Benefits
steps.hint: Explain why these decisions are useful for users.These recommendations benefit users because…

steps.label: Consider limitations
steps.hint: Identify possible weaknesses or risks
steps.starter: However, a potential limitation of these recommendations is…

steps.label: Judgement of Suitability
steps.hint: Make a final judgement about how suitable the decisions are.
steps.starter: Overall, the recommendations produced by the DSS are suitable because…

build.q: Use a top-level IPSO diagram to illustrate how the system will function including knowledge base, rule base, inference process and user inputs.

steps.label: System Overview
steps.hint: Introduce the conceptual structure of the system.
steps.starter: The overall functioning of the DSS can be represented using a top-level IPSO diagram.

steps.label: Inputs Explanation
steps.hint: Identify the data or information users provide.
steps.starter: The inputs to the system include…

steps.label: Process Explanation
steps.hint: Explain how the knowledge base, rule base and inference engine operate.
steps.starter: Within the processing stage, the knowledge base stores…, while the rule base contains…

steps.label: Storage Explanation
steps.hint: Explain where system data is stored.
steps.starter: The system stores information such as…

steps.label: Outputs Explanation
steps.hint: Explain what recommendations the system generates.
steps.starter: The output of the system includes…

build.q: Analyse and evaluate the types of data to be used.

steps.label: Identify Data Types
steps.hint: Introduce the different types of data the system uses.
steps.starter: The DSS will utilise several types of data including…

steps.label: Analyse Data Sources
steps.hint: Explain where the data comes from.
steps.starter: This data will be collected from sources such as…

steps.label: Analyse Role of Data in Decision Making
steps.hint: Explain how the data supports the DSS logic.
steps.starter: This data contributes to decision making by…

steps.label: Evaluate Data Quality
steps.hint: Assess reliability, accuracy and usefulness.
steps.starter: The reliability of this data is important because…

steps.label: Judgement of Suitability
steps.hint: Conclude whether the data is appropriate for the system.
steps.starter: Overall, the selected data sources are suitable because…

build.q: Evaluate relevant social, ethical and legal considerations related to data collection, processing and system design.

steps.label: Introduce Considerations
steps.hint: Introduce the importance of considering impacts when designing the DSS.
steps.starter: When designing this DSS, it is important to consider several social, ethical and legal issues.

steps.label: Social Considerations
steps.hint: Explain how the system may affect users or society.
steps.starter: A key social consideration is…

steps.label: Ethical Considerations
steps.hint: Discuss fairness, bias or responsible use of data.
steps.starter: An important ethical issue is…

steps.label: Legal Considerations
steps.hint: Discuss privacy, intellectual property or data protection laws.
steps.starter: A relevant legal consideration is…

steps.label: Evaluation and Judgement
steps.hint: Evaluate the importance of addressing these issues.
steps.starter: Overall, addressing these considerations is essential because…

grader.q: What is an outfit recommending Decision Support System and how does it help users decide what to wear?
grader.text: An outfit recommending DSS is a system that helps users decide what clothes to wear. It takes inputs such as weather conditions, dress codes and user preferences. The system processes this information and generates a recommended outfit for the user.
grader.explanation:
Why?
The response:
States what the system is
Identifies basic characteristics
Does not explain relationships, break down components, or make judgements
This is typical define-level writing, where the student simply identifies or states the concept.

grader.q: How does an outfit recommending DSS assist users in making clothing decisions?
grader.text: The outfit recommending DSS helps users decide what to wear by analysing several factors that influence clothing choices. These factors include weather conditions, user preferences and dress code requirements. For example, if the weather is cold and the user prefers casual clothing, the system may recommend wearing a jacket, jeans and sneakers. By combining multiple inputs, the DSS helps users make faster and more informed clothing decisions.
grader.explanation:
The response:
Describes how the system works
Shows relationships between inputs and outputs
Gives a simple example scenario
However, it does not break down components deeply or evaluate effectiveness.
This demonstrates explain-level writing.

grader.q: How do existing outfit recommendation systems compare when solving the problem of choosing suitable clothing?
grader.text: Several outfit recommendation systems currently exist, such as weather-based clothing apps and fashion styling platforms [provide links and examples]. These systems typically recommend outfits based on weather forecasts or fashion trends. However, many of these systems do not consider multiple factors simultaneously, such as personal preferences, dress codes and wardrobe availability. As a result, their recommendations may not always be practical for users. Therefore, an outfit recommending DSS that integrates these multiple data sources would provide more accurate and personalised recommendations.
grader.explanation:
The response:
Identifies multiple systems
Breaks down features and limitations
Shows relationships between system design and user needs
Explains why existing systems may not fully solve the problem
This is characteristic of analyse-level responses, where the student examines components and relationships.

grader.q: How suitable are existing outfit recommendation systems for helping users make clothing decisions?
grader.text: Existing outfit recommendation systems provide useful suggestions but often lack personalisation and contextual awareness. When assessed against criteria such as usability, decision accuracy and flexibility, many of these systems rely heavily on fashion trends rather than practical user needs. This can result in recommendations that do not match the user’s available wardrobe or dress code requirements. Therefore, a more advanced DSS that integrates weather data, personal clothing inventory and user preferences would produce more suitable recommendations and improve the overall decision-making process. [Provide visuals/revies etc]
grader.explanation:
The response:
Uses criteria such as usability, accuracy and flexibility
Identifies strengths and weaknesses
Makes a judgement about suitability
Proposes a better DSS approach
This reflects evaluate-level writing, where students assess effectiveness and justify conclusions.
